title: Projects
text:
Working Paper


Research Question: Are subsequent mobility destinations more likely to be cities that were already visible in scientists’ origin-anchored pre-move publication-location environments?
Data: Mobility event × candidate city data for observed intercity moves by elite biomedical scientists.
Methods: Event-specific candidate destination sets, origin-anchored pre-move publication-location exposure, conditional logit, pre-move destination opportunity controls, geographic distance, timing robustness, and city-identity sensitivity checks.
Main Finding: Among observed mobility events, elite biomedical scientists are more likely to select cities that were already visible in their origin-anchored pre-move publication-location environments. The model does not show that exposure produces a move, and it treats publication-location exposure as destination visibility rather than interpersonal collaboration.

title: Projects
section: Global Mobility and Collaboration Networks of Elite Biomedical Scientists
text:
Global Mobility and Collaboration Networks of Elite Biomedical Scientists

Ongoing Research Program

Global Mobility and Collaboration Networks of Elite Biomedical Scientists
Research Question: How do elite biomedical scientists’ career moves, collaboration records, and city-level opportunity structures form spatial networks of scientific work?
Data: A 1970-2023 longitudinal database of 500 elite biomedical scientists, integrating career histories, institutional appointments, publication locations, collaboration records, mobility events, and city attributes.
Methods: Scientist-institution-city linkage, directed weighted mobility networks, centrality, core-periphery structure, K-core, WERGM/ERGM specifications, ego-network measures, and Python/R workflows for temporal auditing and documentation.
Contribution: This program provides the data and measurement foundation for the accepted article, the master’s thesis, and newer destination-choice work while keeping mobility events, publication locations, and collaboration records analytically separate.

title: Projects
section: Intercity Mobility Networks and Career Stages
text:
Intercity Mobility Networks and Career Stages

Accepted Article and Thesis-Related Research

Spatial Structure of Top Biomedical Scientists’ Mobility
Research Question: What spatial structure characterizes global biomedical scientists’ intercity mobility, and how do associated factors differ across career stages?
Data: Career-history and intercity mobility records for top biomedical scientists, with thesis work extending to whole-network and ego-network perspectives.
Methods: Social network analysis, GIS spatial analysis, Heckman two-stage modeling for mobility-tie formation and intensity, and ego-network brokerage/diversity measures.
Contribution: The accepted article emphasizes urban network structure and mobility determinants. The master’s thesis extends the analysis by linking the whole intercity network with selected scientist-level ego networks.

title: Projects
text:
Scopus Research Data Toolkit

Research Software · Python · v0.2.0

Scopus Research Data Toolkit
Research Question: How can Scopus-oriented bibliographic workflows be made reproducible and public-safe when nonpublic records and API responses cannot be released?
Data: Fully synthetic Scopus-style and OpenAlex-style fixtures, deterministic reference outputs, and offline mock transport.
Methods: Author-publication linkage, author-affiliation parsing, city-year publication controls, cognitive-proximity measures, source comparison, schema validation, manifests, and automated tests.
Contribution: The toolkit documents reusable measurement boundaries for publication and affiliation data. It is MIT licensed, includes 39 test functions in the current test suite, and defers live Scopus adapter behavior in v0.2.0. View repository

title: Projects
text:
Selected Earlier Research


Earlier Research

Urban Resilience Measurement from Daily Nighttime Lights
Using VIIRS-DNB daily nighttime-light data for 48 cities in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei, Yangtze River Delta, and Pearl River Delta urban agglomerations, this project constructed high-frequency radiance time series and a Fisher Information Light indicator. Dynamic time warping and K-Means clustering were used to compare short-term urban activity and recovery trajectories.



Ongoing Comparative Research

Talent Mobility and Biomedical Innovation Systems in China and the United States
This project compares China and the United States through top biomedical scientists’ training, career mobility, research output, patent-related information, collaboration networks, and university-industry-research linkages. Current work centers on data organization, biography matching, network construction, and comparative analysis.



Earlier Research

Global Trade Network Evolution during COVID-19
This undergraduate research built directed weighted global trade networks from 2019-2021 UN Comtrade data covering 219 countries and regions. It compared centrality, core structure, product-type differences, and spatial organization before and during the COVID-19 period.

title: Research
text: My research asks how scientific mobility, knowledge production, and regional innovation are organized across space. The empirical focus is elite biomedical scientists, but the broader agenda is economic geography: how people, institutions, publications, and cities form spatial systems of scientific work.

title: Research
text: My research asks how scientific mobility, knowledge production, and regional innovation are organized across space. The empirical focus is elite biomedical scientists, but the broader agenda is economic geography: how people, institutions, publications, and cities form spatial systems of scientific work.

title: Research
text:
Scientific Mobility and Destination Choice
How do particular cities become plausible destinations before an observed move?
I study mobility as a choice among candidate cities, not only as an aggregate flow between places. The destination-choice working paper constructs event-specific candidate sets and measures origin-anchored pre-move publication-location exposure. The interpretation is deliberately limited: among observed mobility events, scientists are more likely to select cities that were already visible in their pre-move publication-location environments, conditional on destination opportunity measures and geographic distance.

title: Research
text:
Knowledge Networks and Regional Innovation
How do scientists’ career trajectories connect individual knowledge production with city and regional innovation systems?
This line of work builds on the master’s thesis, the accepted article, and ongoing research on elite biomedical scientists. It links career histories, institutional appointments, publication locations, collaboration records, mobility events, and city attributes. The goal is to describe how intercity mobility networks, research platforms, and regional opportunity structures are related, while keeping the interpretation within the limits of the research design.

title: Research
section: Longitudinal Relational Data and Spatial Measurement
text:
Longitudinal Relational Data and Spatial Measurement
What kinds of data infrastructure are needed to study scientific careers across people, institutions, publications, and cities?
A large part of my research contribution is measurement work: entity resolution, author-institution-city linkage, candidate-destination construction, city identity checks, and reproducible data-quality audits. The public Scopus Research Data Toolkit reflects this part of the agenda in a safe form, using synthetic fixtures and offline mock transport rather than nonpublic research records.

title: Research
text:
Methods in Use
The main methods include longitudinal multi-source data construction, social and spatial network analysis, ego-network analysis, conditional logit models for event-level destination choice, WERGM/ERGM specifications for network projects, GIS-based spatial measurement, and reproducible Python/R workflows.

title: Ran Wang
text: Ran Wang

title: Ran Wang
text:
Current Research Focus
My current work focuses on elite biomedical scientists as a window into scientific mobility and the geography of knowledge production. One line of research models observed intercity moves as event-level destination choices. A broader line builds scientist-institution-city data to study mobility networks, collaboration records, and regional innovation conditions while keeping these concepts analytically distinct.

title: Ran Wang
text:
Selected Work


Pre-move Publication-Location Exposure and Destination Choice among Elite Biomedical Scientists
Working paper on whether observed destinations were already visible in scientists’ origin-anchored pre-move publication-location environments.


Spatial Structure and Determinants of Top Global Biomedical Scientists’ Mobility from an Urban Network Perspective
Accepted article in World Regional Studies on intercity mobility networks and factors associated with mobility-tie formation and intensity.


Scopus Research Data Toolkit
Public Python toolkit for reproducible Scopus-oriented workflows using synthetic fixtures, offline mock transport, and documented measurement boundaries.

title: Ran Wang
text:
Methods
Longitudinal relational data construction; scientist-institution-city linkage; entity resolution; social and spatial network analysis; ego-network analysis; conditional logit for event-level destination choice; WERGM/ERGM for network projects; Python, R, Stata, GIS, NetworkX, Gephi, and Ucinet.

title: Publications
text:
Accepted Article



城市网络视角下全球生物医药顶尖科学家流动的空间结构与影响因素

Authors: Ran Wang and Jinliao He. Status: Accepted, World Regional Studies.
This article studies the spatial structure of elite biomedical scientists’ intercity mobility from an urban network perspective. It constructs career-history and intercity mobility data and uses social network analysis and a Heckman two-stage model to examine mobility-tie formation and mobility intensity across career stages.

title: Publications
text:
Working Papers

Working Paper

Pre-move Publication-Location Exposure and Destination Choice among Elite Biomedical Scientists
This paper reframes observed intercity moves as event-level destination choices. It constructs event-specific candidate destination sets and origin-anchored pre-move publication-location exposure, then estimates conditional logit models with pre-move destination opportunity controls and geographic distance. The result is interpreted as destination visibility, not as proof that exposure produces a move or as a direct collaboration mechanism.

title: Publications
text:
Master’s Thesis

Master’s Thesis

Intercity Mobility Network of Top Global Biomedical Scientists: A Dual Perspective of Whole and Ego Networks

全球生物医药顶尖科学家城际流动网络——基于全域与自我网络双重视角

The thesis analyzes intercity mobility among top global biomedical scientists through whole-network and ego-network perspectives. It integrates a 1970-2023 longitudinal database of 500 scientists with a 40-scientist ego-network subsample, connecting city-level mobility structure with scientist-level career and collaboration records.

title: Publications
text:
Research Software

Research Software · v0.2.0

Scopus Research Data Toolkit
A modular Python toolkit for reproducible Scopus-oriented research workflows. Using fully synthetic fixtures and offline mock transport, it demonstrates author-publication and author-affiliation linkage, city-year publication controls, cognitive-proximity measures, source comparison, and data-quality audits. The toolkit is MIT licensed and is not an official Scopus or Elsevier tool. View repository

title: CV
text: Ran Wang is a master’s candidate in Human Geography at East China Normal University, with research training in economic geography, regional innovation, scientific mobility, urban networks, and spatial data analysis. Her work uses longitudinal relational data, network analysis, destination-choice models, and reproducible Python/R workflows to study scientific mobility and the spatial organization of knowledge production.

title: CV
text: Ran Wang is a master’s candidate in Human Geography at East China Normal University, with research training in economic geography, regional innovation, scientific mobility, urban networks, and spatial data analysis. Her work uses longitudinal relational data, network analysis, destination-choice models, and reproducible Python/R workflows to study scientific mobility and the spatial organization of knowledge production.
